narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 33, you've shown determination in your academic journey, including ranking in the 8th decile for your first-year grades and 6th decile by your third year—both above average for students in this dataset. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you've indicated plans to attend law school part-time, which is less common as only about 7% of similar students choose that path over full-time. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 53% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—a threshold that unfortunately led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your choice of part-time enrollment, which the model sees as a stronger risk signal compared to the overwhelming majority who go full-time and tend to perform better on the bar. On a more positive note, your strong first-year decile ranking worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other strength in your profile. Your third-year decile also helped somewhat, nudging things toward passing, though with less impact. Overall, while your LSAT is a bit below the typical 36.7 average and your GPA slightly under the 3.21 norm, it was the part-time status that tipped the scales most decisively against you. This doesn't reflect your full potential as a determined student from a middle-income background—it's just how this predictive tool weighed patterns from thousands of past cases. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time commitment if feasible, as that alone could shift the model's view dramatically. Boosting your LSAT through targeted prep might also help, given its relevance in these predictions. You could reach out to admissions for feedback or explore programs with more flexible models for part-time students. Keep pushing; many have overcome similar hurdles to succeed in law.
